text: Here we present a stepwise approach to laparoscopic anterior resection.
Our patient is a sixty-eight year old female with a preoperative T-three N-two M-zero staging.
The tumour can be visualised on these MRI sequences.
The patient is positioned supine with legs raised in the orthotomy position, preparing for the laparoscopic anterior resection procedure.
The surgeon places laparoscopic ports according to the displayed diagram, preparing for the anterior resection procedure.
The patient is positioned into reverse Trendelenburg with the right shoulder tilted down after port placement, preparing for the laparoscopic anterior resection.
The small bowel and omentum are moved into the right and upper parts of the abdomen, providing exposure to the relevant anatomical structures for the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the groove of the inferior mesenteric artery, preparing for further dissection in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon makes an initial incision in the peritoneum at the level of the pelvic brim, preparing to access deeper pelvic structures.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection as they progress towards the vessel, carefully separating tissue layers in the exposed surgical field.
Care needs to be taken to avoid damaging important structures such as the ureter and the hypogastric nerves posteriorly.
The surgeon identifies and skeletalizes the vessel, revealing its structure within the surgical field.
The surgeon highlights the proximity of the ureter during the dissection, ensuring careful manipulation to avoid damage to this structure.
Hemlock clips are applied to the vessel, which is then divided approximately one centimeter from its origin, following the initial incision in the peritoneum at the pelvic brim.
The surgeon performs further medial to lateral dissection, separating the retroperitoneal structures from the mesocolon, ensuring careful manipulation to avoid damage to nearby anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies and divides the inferior mesenteric vein at the lower border of the pancreas, a crucial step to prevent traction injury during dissection over the pancreas.
The surgeon creates a window over the pancreas, providing access to the lesser sac.
A swab is placed in the surgical field to serve as a visual landmark during the mobilization of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the lateral wall, separating the mesocolon from the retroperitoneal structures.
The surgeon places a second swab as a visual marker to guide the lateral mobilization of the colon.
The surgeon demonstrates optimum lateral mobilization by applying traction and counter traction techniques, facilitating the separation of tissue layers during the dissection process.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the established line until reaching the swab placed as a landmark during the previous segment, indicating the progress of lateral mobilization.
The surgeon begins mobilization of the splenic flexure by separating the gastrocolic ligament, entering the lesser sac. A swab acts as a landmark during this process.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection towards the splenic flexure, progressing along the established line of dissection with visible tissue separation.
The surgeon divides the splenocolic ligament, continuing the mobilization process of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon points out the swab placed over the pancreas, visible posteriorly, as part of the ongoing dissection process.
The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization, uniting it with the window created over the pancreas, marked by the swab placed in the previous segment.

The surgeon highlights the proximity of the stomach during the mobilization of the splenic flexure, with visible anatomical landmarks indicating the close spatial relationship between the stomach and the mobilized structures.
The surgeon uses counter traction to straighten the splenic flexure, uniting the superior, lateral, and posterior portions of the dissection, thereby fully mobilizing the splenic flexure.
The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision using a monopolar hook diathermy, carefully dissecting the mesorectal tissue from surrounding structures.
The rectum is visualized as a four-sided box, with clearly defined anterior, posterior, left, and right lateral sides.
The surgeon begins the posterior dissection above the presacral fascia, extending the dissection down towards the pelvic floor.
The surgeon continues the posterior dissection above the presacral fascia, extending the dissection as far as possible towards the pelvic floor.
Care must be taken not to dissect too posteriorly
which would risk injuring the presacral vessels.
The surgeon completes the posterior dissection and begins lateral mobilization of the rectum, focusing on separating the lateral tissue planes while maintaining the four-sided box configuration.
The surgeon begins the dissection two centimeters above the peritoneal reflection, continuing the procedure within the pelvic region.
Again, we would emphasize the importance of good traction and counter traction.
The surgeon continues the lateral dissection and unites it with the anterior dissection, ensuring careful manipulation of the tissue to avoid injury to surrounding structures.
The surgeon elevates the uterus using a 2-0 Prolene stitch, providing better access to the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the anterior dissection after uniting the two lateral dissections, progressing the surgical approach in the pelvic region.
Care at this point should be taken to avoid injuring the posterior vaginal wall.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to perform a precise dissection, with the diaphragm visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon applies optimum traction and counter traction while using the monopolar hook diaphragm for precise dissection, ensuring careful manipulation of the tissue.
Following the completion of the initial circumferential mobilization, the surgeon proceeds with deeper dissection towards the pelvic floor.
The surgeon performs lateral mobilization, carefully avoiding injury to the visible lateral pelvic nerves during the dissection.
The lateral pelvic nerves are visible near the plane of dissection, highlighting their proximity during the surgical procedure.
The surgeon manipulates the rectum to free any remaining elements of the mesorectum, ensuring complete mobilization within the pelvic cavity.
Following the completion of the total mesorectal excision (TME), the surgeon begins the transection of the rectal tube using surgical instruments.
The surgeon divides the rectum, completing the transection process following the circumferential mobilization and dissection towards the pelvic floor.
The surgeon uses an Echelon 45 stapler to transect the rectal tube, requiring two firings to complete the division.
Once the rectum has been removed,
The camera view reveals important anatomical structures within and surrounding the pelvis, providing a clear visual of the surgical field.
The surgeon forms the anastomosis using a CDH-XXIX circular stapling device, connecting the divided ends of the rectal tube.
The trocar emerges from the midpoint of the staple line, which runs in an anterior to posterior direction across the surgical field.
The surgeon extracts the resected specimen through a small midline incision, following the completion of the rectal removal.
The surgeon checks the orientation and tension of the bowel within the pelvis before firing the stapler, ensuring it is correctly positioned for the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a flexible sigmoidoscope to perform a standard leak test, visually inspecting the integrity of the anastomosis within the pelvic region.
The patient was also defunctioned with loop ileostomy and pelvic drain was inserted.
